saida_bruta:
Seeing the boys approach the hill, she watched them hesitate and murmur to one another. "You ask her, Niel."

"You should do it, George. She visits your house constantly, whereas she hardly even knows me."

As they lingered by the three steps leading to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door and greeted them with a cordial nod, cradling a pink rose.

"Good morning, boys. Are you off on a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward, gravely removing his wide straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please wade and fish in the marsh, and eat our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. You have a beautiful day for it. When did school let out? Don't you miss it? I expect Niel does. Judge Pommeroy mentions that he is quite studious."

The boys chuckled, while Niel looked visibly embarrassed.

"Run along then, and ensure you do not leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester dislikes it when the cattle wander into his bluegrass."

The boys slipped quietly around the house toward the grove gate, then raced shouting down the grassy, tree-shaded slopes. Mrs. Forrester observed them from her kitchen window until they vanished over the crest of the hill. She then addressed her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you are baking this morning, please make a tray of cookies for the boys. I will carry them down when they stop for lunch."

saida_bruta:
Observing the boys draw near the hill, she watched them pause and whisper. "You ask her, Niel."

"No, you, George. She's always over at your place, but she hardly knows me."

As they hovered by the trio of front porch steps, Mrs. Forrester emerged at the door, holding a pink rose and offering a friendly nod. 

"Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped up, politely taking off his broad straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please wade and fish in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course you may. It's a lovely day for it. When did school break up? Are you playing hooky? I imagine Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the student."

The boys snickered, which left Niel looking deeply self-conscious.

"Off you go, and be sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester can’t stand the cattle getting into his bluegrass."

The boys crept silently around the house to the grove gate, then bolted shouting down the grassy, shaded hill. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the rise, then addressed her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you are baking this morning, please whip up some cookies for the boys. I'll carry them down when they stop for lunch."

Observing the boys draw near the hill, she watched them pause and whisper. "You ask her, Niel."

"No, you, George. She's always over at your place, but she hardly knows me."

As they hovered by the trio of front porch steps, Mrs. Forrester emerged at the door, holding a pink rose and offering a friendly nod. 

"Good morning, boys. Heading out for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped up, politely taking off his broad straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please wade and fish in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course you may. It's a lovely day for it. When did school break up? Are you playing hooky? I imagine Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he's quite the student."

saida_bruta:
Watching the boys approach the hill, she saw them falter and begin to whisper. "You ask her, Niel."

"No, you, George. She's always over at your house, but she barely knows me."

As they lingered near the three front steps, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, holding a pink rose and greeting them with a warm nod.

"Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward, politely removing his wide straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please wade and fish in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course you may. It is a beautiful day for it. When did school let out? Or are you playing hooky? I suspect Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is a wonderful student."

The boys snickered, leaving Niel looking thoroughly embarrassed.

"Run along, then, and make sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester hates when the cattle get into his bluegrass."

The boys tiptoed quietly around the house to the grove gate, then burst out shouting as they ran down the grassy, shaded slope. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished over the crest, then turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you are baking this morning, please make some cookies for the boys. I will take them down when they stop for lunch."

“Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic?”

“Of course you may. It is a beautiful day for it. When did school let out? Or are you playing hooky? I suspect Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is a wonderful student.”

The boys snickered, leaving Niel looking thoroughly embarrassed.

“Run along, then, and make sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester hates when the cattle get into his bluegrass.”

Tiptoeing quietly around the house to the grove gate, the boys burst into shouts as they ran down the grassy, shaded slope. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished over the crest, then turned to her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, while you are baking this morning, please make some cookies for the boys. I will take them down when they stop for lunch.”

"No, you, George. She is always at your house, whereas she barely knows me."

As they lingered by the front steps, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, holding a pink rose and greeting them with a warm nod.

"Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward, politely removing his wide-brimmed straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please wade and fish in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course you may. It is a beautiful day for it. When did school let out? Or are you playing hooky? I suspect Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is a wonderful student."

The boys giggled, leaving Niel looking thoroughly embarrassed.

"Run along, then, and make sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester hates when the cattle get into his bluegrass."

Tiptoeing quietly around the house to the grove gate, the boys burst into shouts as they ran down the grassy, shaded slope. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished over the crest, then turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, while you are baking this morning, please make some cookies for the boys. I will take them down when they stop for lunch."
